payload.summary: Evaluated five amendments against the workflow composition engine and supporting infrastructure. Three address real bugs or safety-critical inconsistencies (b2220aa1, 39fd79cd, a210d3ba), one addresses minor but real memory accumulation (fd63d152), and one addresses test fragility (3303d1eb). All five are accepted as they identify genuine defects or robustness gaps that should be fixed.

votes.rationale: Technically correct: the HashMap key includes cycle number so old entries are functionally dead but never reclaimed. In runs with many remediation cycles, entries accumulate needlessly. The fix (retain only entries >= current_cycle) is minimal, consistent with CompletionRoundAdvanced's .clear() pattern at line 5865, and has no risk of changing resume semantics since lookups already include the cycle number. Low impact but zero-risk cleanup.

The apply_jitter function uses SystemTime::now().subsec_nanos() % 1000 which yields only 1000 distinct values and can produce identical results on systems with coarse clock granularity (VMs, containers). Since consecutive retries of the same stage may call this within the same millisecond, jitter can collapse to zero diversity, defeating thundering-herd mitigation. Mixing in the attempt number is the simplest fix (no new direct dependency needed). The rand crate is only transitive via quinn-proto, so thread_rng() would require adding it as a direct dep—attempt-number mixing or Instant::now() is preferable.

The test's correctness depends on Tokio's auto-advance implementation detail: that when stdout/stderr EOF completes and only the wait() future remains alongside timers, auto-advance kicks in. This behavior is not guaranteed across Tokio versions. The minimal fix—adding a comment explaining why start_paused works here—is cheap and prevents future confusion when Tokio upgrades. The alternative (real 2-second timeout) is more robust but slower. Either fix is acceptable; the amendment correctly identifies fragile coupling.

This is a real bug. When a stage exhausts retries (will_retry=false after prior will_retry=true failures), the retryable_failed_attempts entry for that stage+cycle is never cleared. On resume, the stale entry causes resume_attempt = old_attempt + 1 instead of 1. SC-RESUME-005 explicitly asserts attempt=1 on resume after terminal failure (lines 4634-4642 of scenarios.rs). The StageFailed handler (lines 5825-5841) only inserts on will_retry=true but never removes on will_retry=false. Fix: add an else branch that calls retryable_failed_attempts.remove(&(stage_id, cycle)) when will_retry is false.

The stage_failed event with will_retry=true is durably appended at lines 4563-4600 BEFORE the snapshot write at lines 4643-4656. If the snapshot write fails (treated as best-effort with only a warn log) and the process then crashes during backoff, the journal records 'failed, retrying' but run.json still shows Running status—making the run non-resumable. This is precisely the crash-during-backoff scenario the snapshot was introduced to prevent. The best-effort comment (lines 4646-4650) acknowledges the failure mode but dismisses it as 'same state as before this snapshot was introduced,' which contradicts the feature's purpose. The write should either propagate the error to abort cleanly, or retry the write before falling back.
